text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY CLINIC NOTE
Genitourinary Oncology Service

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: GU Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established patient follow-up — systemic therapy monitoring

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Margaret Okonkwo is a 64-year-old Nigerian-American woman with metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), presenting today for her scheduled medical oncology follow-up at approximately 9 months on cabozantinib therapy (approximately 18 months from original diagnosis). She is here for comprehensive reassessment of disease status, review of recent imaging, laboratory results, and ongoing side effect management.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo is well known to our GU oncology service. She was originally diagnosed with stage IV ccRCC approximately 18 months ago when she presented with symptomatic right flank pain and gross hematuria. Workup revealed a large right renal mass (14.2 cm, T3b with renal vein tumor thrombus), right retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (N1), and bilateral pulmonary nodules consistent with metastatic disease. Histology was notable for Fuhrman grade 4 clear cell RCC with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation. Tumor NGS demonstrated VHL, PBRM1, and SETD2 mutations, as well as TP53 mutation and MET high-level amplification. PD-L1 CPS was 12. TMB was low. IMDC intermediate risk.

Her oncologic course has been as follows:
- Underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection approximately 6 weeks after diagnosis, achieving R0 resection. Pathology confirmed pT3b pN1 M1, Stage IV. Postoperative course was complicated by a brief grade 2 ileus, which resolved.
- Approximately 6 weeks post-nephrectomy, she initiated first-line therapy with ipilimumab 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks per the CheckMate 214 regimen, given IMDC intermediate risk and sarcomatoid histology (pre-specified subgroup with improved benefit from dual checkpoint blockade). After cycle 2, she developed grade 2 immune-related colitis requiring ipilimumab discontinuation and a prolonged prednisone taper over 8 weeks. Nivolumab was continued as maintenance per shared decision-making. She additionally developed subclinical immune-related hypothyroidism and was started on levothyroxine.
- Restaging imaging at 3 months showed a mixed response: the dominant right lower lobe (RLL) pulmonary nodule increased slightly (raising the question of pseudoprogression), while smaller nodules decreased. She was continued on nivolumab with close surveillance.
- Confirmatory imaging at 6 months demonstrated unambiguous progression of the dominant RLL nodule, two new right middle lobe (RML) nodules, and a new right paracaval lymph node, confirming true progression. Nivolumab was discontinued.
- She was transitioned to second-line cabozantinib 60 mg orally once daily approximately 9 months after her initial systemic therapy start. Cabozantinib was selected given progression on prior immunotherapy, the presence of MET amplification on NGS (cabozantinib is a dual VEGFR2/MET inhibitor), and aggressive sarcomatoid histology. Early in her cabozantinib course, she developed grade 2 HFSR with blistering, and the dose was reduced to 40 mg daily. She subsequently developed grade 2 TKI-related diarrhea and new-onset grade 2 hypertension, both managed with loperamide/dietary modification and amlodipine dose escalation (5 mg to 10 mg), respectively.
- At her 3-month cabozantinib assessment, restaging CT showed stable disease with a modest reduction in all target lesions (sum of diameters -14% from cabozantinib baseline).
- At 6 months on cabozantinib, continued slow reduction across all target and non-target lesions was noted, with near-complete response of the retroperitoneal lymph node. MRI brain was negative.
- At her most recent interval visit approximately 6 weeks ago (9 months on cabozantinib), she presented with new low back pain. MRI lumbar spine confirmed multilevel degenerative disc disease without osseous metastases. The restaging CT performed concurrently confirmed ongoing stable disease with continued gradual tumor reduction. The dominant RLL nodule measured 2.0 cm (down from 2.6 cm at cabozantinib initiation baseline). Sum of target lesion diameters was 2.7 cm, a 27% decrease from cabozantinib baseline, approaching but not yet meeting formal partial response threshold.

Today she presents for her comprehensive 9-month cabozantinib reassessment. She reports that back pain has substantially improved with physical therapy (6 sessions completed) and intermittent naproxen. She continues to take cabozantinib 40 mg daily without missed doses. She feels her overall energy level is good. She has returned to light gardening, which she finds enjoyable and meaningful. She denies new or worsening symptoms.

Current symptoms:
- Fatigue: Grade 1 (mild, does not significantly impact function)
- HFSR: Describes mild skin thickening on palms and soles, non-painful; applying urea cream regularly
- Diarrhea: 1-2 loose stools above baseline most days, occasional, takes loperamide as needed with good control
- Back pain: Substantially improved; now minimal at rest, mild with prolonged activity; not using naproxen daily
- Appetite: Good, weight stable
- No nausea, no vomiting
- No new rashes, mucositis, or oral sores
- No dyspnea or new respiratory symptoms
- No dysuria or hematuria (expected, solitary left kidney)
- No edema
- No headache or new neurological symptoms

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Reports mild chronic fatigue (grade 1), weight stable. Appetite preserved. No fevers, no night sweats.
Respiratory: No dyspnea at rest or exertion. No cough. No hemoptysis.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain. No palpitations. No lower extremity swelling. Blood pressure managed per home monitoring.
Gastrointestinal: As above — intermittent loose stools, no blood, no mucus, no pain with defecation. Back pain improved.
Genitourinary: No gross hematuria. Urine output adequate. No dysuria.
Musculoskeletal: Low back pain substantially improved.
Neurological: No headache, no focal weakness, no vision changes, no cognitive changes.
Endocrine: No symptoms of hypo- or hyperthyroidism on current levothyroxine dose.
Skin: Mild hyperkeratosis bilateral palms and soles (HFSR grade 1), no blistering, no new skin changes.
Psychiatric: Patient reports overall positive mood. Engaged with care. No depression or anxiety identified.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (previously documented, confirmed today)
1. Metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), stage IV — as above
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — well-controlled on metformin; last HbA1c 7.1%
3. Hypertension — previously on amlodipine 5 mg; dose escalated to 10 mg in setting of cabozantinib-related hypertension
4. Immune-related hypothyroidism (secondary to ipilimumab/nivolumab) — on levothyroxine
5. Immune-related colitis, grade 2 (resolved) — prior ipilimumab permanently discontinued
6. Lumbar spondylosis, L4-L5 (degenerative, no osseous metastases confirmed)
7. Mild chronic anemia of chronic disease/cancer

SOCIAL HISTORY (confirmed)
Ms. Okonkwo is a retired registered nurse with 30 years of clinical experience, predominantly in general medicine and ICU settings. She is a lifelong non-smoker. She does not use alcohol beyond occasional social use (1 drink per week). She denies any illicit drug use. She is widowed, lives independently in her own home. She has two adult children who are supportive and often accompany her to appointments. She is engaged in her care and demonstrates excellent health literacy given her nursing background. She does her own medication management and home blood pressure monitoring.

FAMILY HISTORY (confirmed)
Mother: deceased, breast cancer (age 71). Father: deceased, myocardial infarction. No first-degree relatives with RCC, VHL syndrome, or other hereditary cancer syndromes. Germline testing performed at diagnosis was negative for VHL, FLCN, SDHB/C/D, MET, and PTEN.

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies (NKDA).

MEDICATIONS
1. Cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily (dose-reduced from 60 mg due to grade 2 HFSR)
2. Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally once daily (in the morning, fasting)
3. Metformin 500 mg orally twice daily
4. Amlodipine 10 mg orally once daily
5. Urea 40% cream — apply to bilateral palms and soles twice daily
6. Loperamide 2 mg orally as needed (after each loose stool, max 8 mg/day)
7. Naproxen 500 mg orally twice daily as needed for back pain (used intermittently)
8. Ferrous sulfate 325 mg orally once daily (for chronic anemia)
9. Calcium carbonate 500 mg + vitamin D3 400 IU orally twice daily

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs:
- Blood pressure: 134/80 mmHg (right arm, seated)
- Heart rate: 72 bpm
- Temperature: 36.7°C
- Respiratory rate: 16 breaths/min
- SpO2: 98% on room air
- Weight: 78.2 kg (BMI 28.6, down very slightly from prior; clinically stable)
- ECOG performance status: 1

General: Ms. Okonkwo is a well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. She is alert and oriented, engaging in conversation readily. She appears her stated age.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae non-icteric. Oropharynx clear. No oral mucositis. No lymphadenopathy in the cervical or supraclavicular regions.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema. Peripheral pulses intact bilaterally.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, no rales, no rhonchi. Breathing unlabored.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Right flank well-healed surgical scar, no tenderness at nephrectomy site. No palpable masses. No hepatosplenomegaly. Bowel sounds active.

Musculoskeletal: No costovertebral angle tenderness bilaterally. Lumbar spine with mild tenderness to palpation at L4-L5 paravertebral muscles, improved from prior visit. Full range of motion of lower extremities. No joint effusions.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x 4. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in all four extremities. No focal deficits. Gait steady and normal.

Skin/Extremities: Bilateral palms: mild diffuse hyperkeratosis without erythema, blistering, or active fissuring (Grade 1 HFSR, asymptomatic). Bilateral plantar surfaces: mild hyperkeratosis with slight thickening, non-tender to palpation. No new skin lesions. No rash elsewhere.

Lymph nodes: No cervical, axillary, inguinal, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy detected.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 5.8 x10^9/L (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 10.6 g/dL (mild anemia, Grade 1; stable compared to prior)
- MCV: 87 fL
- Platelets: 202 x10^9/L (normal)
- ANC: 2.9 x10^9/L (normal)
- Lymphocytes: 1.4 x10^9/L (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
- Chloride: 101 mEq/L
- Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
- BUN: 22 mg/dL
- Creatinine: 1.45 mg/dL (eGFR approximately 42 mL/min/1.73m2, CKD stage 3a, consistent with prior values post-right nephrectomy; stable)
- Glucose: 108 mg/dL (mildly elevated, consistent with type 2 DM)
- Total protein: 7.2 g/dL
- Albumin: 3.9 g/dL
- Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
- AST: 28 U/L (normal)
- ALT: 31 U/L (normal)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 72 U/L (normal)
- LDH: 195 U/L (mildly elevated, upper normal range, stable)
- Calcium: 9.2 mg/dL (corrected 9.3 mg/dL, normal)

Thyroid Function:
- TSH: 3.5 mIU/L (normal; euthyroid on levothyroxine 75 mcg — dose appropriate, no adjustment needed)
- Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal)

Urinalysis:
- Color: yellow, clear
- Protein: trace (no significant proteinuria)
- Glucose: negative
- Blood: negative
- No casts
Note: Absence of significant proteinuria is reassuring given ongoing VEGFR inhibitor therapy.

Lipid panel (performed this visit per endocrine monitoring): Total cholesterol 182 mg/dL, LDL 108 mg/dL, HDL 52 mg/dL, triglycerides 118 mg/dL — all within acceptable range.

HbA1c: 7.1% (adequate glycemic control, stable; metformin continued).

---

IMAGING RESULTS

MRI Lumbar Spine (reviewed — obtained approximately 6 weeks ago):
Multilevel degenerative disc disease most pronounced at L4-L5 with moderate disc height loss and facet arthropathy. No vertebral metastatic lesions identified. No lytic or sclerotic lesions. No pathological fractures. No epidural extension or cord compression. Mild bilateral L4-L5 foraminal narrowing from hypertrophic facet changes. Findings fully consistent with degenerative disease in a 64-year-old woman with a history of nursing (long periods of standing). No oncologic concern on this study.

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with Contrast (restaging, reviewed — obtained approximately 6 weeks ago, 9-month mark on cabozantinib):
All target pulmonary lesions showing continued gradual reduction. The dominant right lower lobe nodule has decreased to 2.0 cm from its nadir of 2.6 cm at the time cabozantinib was started — this lesion, which had grown on nivolumab, has now been slowly but steadily responding to cabozantinib for 9 months. The second RLL nodule is 0.4 cm. The left lower lobe nodule is 0.3 cm. The right middle lobe nodules that appeared as new disease on nivolumab have shrunk to 0.3 cm and 0.2 cm. The right paracaval node, which was 1.1 cm at the time of progression on nivolumab, is no longer measurable — essentially a near-complete nodal response. Sum of measurable target lesions is approximately 2.7 cm, representing a 27% reduction from the cabozantinib starting point. No new pulmonary nodules. No hepatic lesions. No adrenal lesions. No osseous metastases. No new retroperitoneal adenopathy. No pleural effusion. Nephrectomy bed is clean without local recurrence.

Overall imaging impression: Stable disease with continued slow but meaningful response across all sites. This is a patient who has now maintained disease control for 9 months on cabozantinib, with the dominant lesion shrinking from 2.6 cm to 2.0 cm, and near-complete response of previously progressive retroperitoneal nodal disease. No new sites of disease identified.

---

QUALITY OF LIFE ASSESSMENT

FKSI-DRS (Functional Assessment of Cancer Therapy — Kidney Symptom Index, Disease Related Symptoms) administered today. Patient's total score reflects improvement from the 6-month assessment. She reports better energy, less pain, and less worry about disease worsening. She endorses meaningful engagement in activities she values (gardening, time with family). No significant emotional distress detected. Patient declined referral to palliative care social work at this time but remains open to consultation if circumstances change.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Okonkwo is a 64-year-old woman with metastatic clear cell RCC (grade 4, sarcomatoid differentiation, MET-amplified, VHL/PBRM1/SETD2 mutant) who has completed a cytoreductive nephrectomy followed by first-line ipi/nivo (complicated by grade 2 immune-related colitis leading to ipi discontinuation, ultimately progressed at 6 months on nivo monotherapy), and is currently on second-line cabozantinib 40 mg daily. Today's visit represents a comprehensive 9-month assessment.

1. METASTATIC ccRCC — STABLE DISEASE ON CABOZANTINIB
Patient is deriving durable, ongoing benefit from cabozantinib with 9 months of disease control and continued slow tumor reduction across all sites. The dominant right lower lobe pulmonary nodule has decreased from 2.6 cm to 2.0 cm. The previously progressive retroperitoneal lymph node has essentially disappeared. No new sites of disease have emerged since cabozantinib initiation. While formal RECIST partial response criteria have not yet been met (the sum of target lesions has fallen 27%, just below the 30% threshold), this is clinically meaningful disease control in a patient with aggressive histology (sarcomatoid, grade 4, MET-amplified). MET amplification was noted on NGS at diagnosis; cabozantinib's dual inhibition of VEGFR2 and MET may be contributing to the sustained response in this case.
- Plan: Continue cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily. No dose escalation back to 60 mg at this time — patient prefers to defer given Grade 1 HFSR, and the current dose is achieving disease control. This is a reasonable approach; dose re-escalation can be revisited if HFSR fully resolves.
- Next restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in approximately 3 months.
- No role for bone-modifying agents at present (no osseous metastases identified; MRI spine negative).
- No active clinical trial eligibility identified at this institution currently, but patient will be re-evaluated at each visit. Discussed the evolving landscape of post-IO/VEGFR therapy in RCC.

2. HAND-FOOT SKIN REACTION (HFSR) — GRADE 1, STABLE
Prior grade 2 HFSR prompted dose reduction from cabozantinib 60 mg to 40 mg. On the reduced dose with urea 40% cream applied regularly, HFSR has downgraded to grade 1 with no functional limitation. Palmar and plantar hyperkeratosis remains present but asymptomatic.
- Plan: Continue urea 40% cream twice daily to bilateral palms and soles. Reinforce use of cushioned footwear, avoidance of tight-fitting shoes, and moisture-wicking gloves for gardening. No further dose adjustment needed at grade 1. If HFSR worsens to grade 2 again, revisit management. Dermatology follow-up as needed.

3. TKI-RELATED DIARRHEA — GRADE 1, WELL-CONTROLLED
Patient continues to have intermittent 1-2 loose stools above baseline, well-managed with as-needed loperamide and a largely low-residue diet. No grade 2 diarrhea since the initial weeks of cabozantinib. Infectious causes excluded at prior evaluation.
- Plan: Continue PRN loperamide. Reinforce dietary modifications. Patient educated on when to call with worsening (more than 4 stools above baseline, blood in stool, dehydration symptoms).

4. HYPERTENSION — WELL-CONTROLLED
Blood pressure today 134/80 mmHg on amlodipine 10 mg. Previously required dose escalation from 5 mg to 10 mg due to cabozantinib-related hypertension. Now well-controlled and stable. Home BP log reviewed — readings consistently in the 130s/80s range over past 4 weeks.
- Plan: Continue amlodipine 10 mg daily. Continue home BP monitoring twice daily. If BP rises above 150/100 mmHg on repeated home readings, contact clinic for further management. No additional antihypertensive needed at this time. Reviewed low-sodium dietary strategies.

5. IMMUNE-RELATED HYPOTHYROIDISM (RESOLVED SYMPTOMATICALLY, ON REPLACEMENT)
Euthyroid on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH 3.5 mIU/L, free T4 normal. Dose was appropriately adjusted upward from 50 mcg to 75 mcg at the 15-month mark. Patient has no symptoms of hypothyroidism.
- Plan: Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg. Recheck TSH in 3 months with next laboratory assessment.

6. CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE / RENAL FUNCTION MONITORING
Creatinine 1.45 mg/dL (eGFR ~42 mL/min/1.73m2), stable since nephrectomy. Patient is functioning on a solitary left kidney. No significant proteinuria on urinalysis today. Metformin is being used cautiously given CKD; current eGFR is above the threshold for temporary discontinuation.
- Plan: Continue monitoring creatinine every 3 months. Avoid nephrotoxic agents where possible. Patient cautioned on NSAID use (naproxen for back pain) — she should limit this to the minimum effective dose and duration. Discuss with PCP regarding renal monitoring as well.

7. BACK PAIN / LUMBAR SPONDYLOSIS
Back pain substantially improved with physical therapy and intermittent naproxen. MRI confirmed this is degenerative disc disease at L4-L5 only, with no osseous metastases or epidural involvement.
- Plan: Continue physical therapy as needed. Naproxen on an as-needed basis with the above cautions regarding renal function. Patient given referral for continued outpatient PT if she desires additional sessions. If back pain worsens, recurs, or develops any new radicular features or neurological symptoms, will re-image promptly.

8. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS
HbA1c 7.1% — adequate control. Fasting glucose 108 mg/dL. Patient on metformin 500 mg twice daily. Weight stable. No signs of peripheral neuropathy.
- Plan: Continue metformin. PCP to co-manage DM. Reinforce dietary adherence.

9. MILD CHRONIC ANEMIA
Hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL, stable. This is a multifactorial anemia in the context of metastatic disease, chronic kidney disease (erythropoietin deficiency contribution), and TKI therapy. On oral iron supplementation.
- Plan: Continue ferrous sulfate. No transfusion indicated. Recheck CBC in 3 months. If hemoglobin falls below 9 g/dL, will reassess and consider further workup including EPO level.

---

PATIENT EDUCATION AND COUNSELING

Extensive time spent with Ms. Okonkwo reviewing her disease status, the significance of stable disease and continued tumor reduction on cabozantinib, and the current treatment plan. She understands that while her disease is not cured, she is maintaining excellent control and tolerance of therapy. We discussed the importance of continued adherence to cabozantinib — specifically taking it on an empty stomach or with a low-fat meal, avoiding grapefruit juice and strong CYP3A4 inhibitors, and taking it at the same time each day. She was reminded to call the oncology nurse line for: fever > 38.3°C, more than 4 loose stools per day, severe worsening of hand-foot symptoms, chest pain, or any new neurological symptoms.

Given her background as a retired nurse, she participates actively in her own care and consistently demonstrates understanding of her condition, her medications, and their rationale. She declined palliative care referral for now but was encouraged to engage whenever she feels it would be helpful. She expressed gratitude for the care received and optimism about continuing on her current therapy.

---

FOLLOW-UP

- Next medical oncology visit in 6 weeks for interim assessment and laboratory review
- Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in approximately 3 months
- TSH and CMP in 3 months
- Continue physical therapy follow-up for back pain as needed
- PCP co-management of hypertension, diabetes, and CKD ongoing
- Dermatology follow-up for HFSR as needed

Total time with patient: 55 minutes (greater than 50% counseling and coordination of care).

Electronically signed by:
Attending, Genitourinary Medical Oncology
